title: Jesus’ love for his Father part 1
scripture_text: ‘’After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you. For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’
devotional:
John 17 is the longest prayer of Jesus in the bible, which can be divided up into three parts. In verses 1-5 Jesus prays for himself, in verses 6-19 he prays for his disciples and in verses 20-26 he prays for the whole church.

So we want to look at the first part of this prayer.

Before we do that let me say that there are a number of reasons why we should consider these verses.

1. These verses encapsulate Jesus’ relationship with his father. If we want to know what it really means to love God, then these verses will help us. As Jesus prays, He reveals his heart, He shows us how He lived and what He lived for. Understanding these verses will help us understand Jesus. The things we pray about ourselves, say a lot about who we are and what we are living for.

2. These verses will help us to love the father in the same way Jesus did. I have been thinking a lot recently about how wonderful it would be if we could have the same intense love for God our father that Jesus had.

Why are we so content with having an insipid, cold, predictable love for God? Why are we so conditioned to settle for a mediocre love for God? We should have a desire and an aspiration to love our Father God in the same way Jesus did. You may be thinking, that is impossible, Jesus was different, he was God. You’re right, but it was Jesus’ intense, fervent love for his father that made him human. Let me put this way. The more we love God, the more human we become. Not to love God with all our hearts is to deny who we really are, and a refusal to be who we should be.

3. These verses teach us that intimacy with God is not a luxury but an essential. Sadly so many people start their walk with Jesus well, but end badly, or not at all. They give up, they walk away, they jettison their faith; one of the reasons for this, [maybe the main reason.] is that they are trying to live a Christian life devoid of any intimacy with God. Think about this, trying to survive as a Christian, and remaining faithful to God is impossible without intimacy. Take intimacy out of our relationship with God, and you end up reducing Christianity to a set of cerebral dogmas that only require unemotional compliance, not the pursuit of a loving relationship.

4. These verses teach us that Jesus’ life was defined by his relationship with his Father. At some level we choose what defines us. For example, I could choose to live as a disabled person. I could allow my disability to determine how I should view life, and control the choices I make.

Or sometimes we can be defined by the hurts of the past, and we view ourselves as victims. Or we can be defined by our success. We may look at what we have achieved and develop a heart full of pride.

But Jesus’ life teaches us, that healthy, wholesome humanity can only be defined in the context of the fatherhood of God.

What I am trying to say is, that our relationship with God the Father should be the main thing that explains who we are. As Jesus’ relationship with his father was the explanation for his life, so it should be with us.

5. The relationship Jesus had with Father gives us a practical demonstration of ‘eternal life.’

Many people have a wrong understanding of eternal life, often eternal life is viewed as a life that lasts for eternity, which is just one aspect of it, and in some ways the least important aspect. Eternal life is knowing God in an intimate way. It is a quality of life that is the result of continually growing in your knowledge and experience of God.

Over the next few days, let us look at these verses and try and understand the relationship Jesus had with his Father.
prayer: Father give me a capacity to love you like Jesus.
html:
Jesus’ love for his Father part 1

Study Text: John 17 v 1-5
‘’After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you. For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’

John 17 is the longest prayer of Jesus in the bible, which can be divided up into three parts. In verses 1-5 Jesus prays for himself, in verses 6-19 he prays for his disciples and in verses 20-26 he prays for the whole church.

So we want to look at the first part of this prayer.

Before we do that let me say that there are a number of reasons why we should consider these verses.

1. These verses encapsulate Jesus’ relationship with his father. If we want to know what it really means to love God, then these verses will help us. As Jesus prays, He reveals his heart, He shows us how He lived and what He lived for. Understanding these verses will help us understand Jesus. The things we pray about ourselves, say a lot about who we are and what we are living for.

2. These verses will help us to love the father in the same way Jesus did. I have been thinking a lot recently about how wonderful it would be if we could have the same intense love for God our father that Jesus had.

Why are we so content with having an insipid, cold, predictable love for God? Why are we so conditioned to settle for a mediocre love for God? We should have a desire and an aspiration to love our Father God in the same way Jesus did. You may be thinking, that is impossible, Jesus was different, he was God. You’re right, but it was Jesus’ intense, fervent love for his father that made him human. Let me put this way. The more we love God, the more human we become. Not to love God with all our hearts is to deny who we really are, and a refusal to be who we should be.

3. These verses teach us that intimacy with God is not a luxury but an essential. Sadly so many people start their walk with Jesus well, but end badly, or not at all. They give up, they walk away, they jettison their faith; one of the reasons for this, [maybe the main reason.] is that they are trying to live a Christian life devoid of any intimacy with God. Think about this, trying to survive as a Christian, and remaining faithful to God is impossible without intimacy. Take intimacy out of our relationship with God, and you end up reducing Christianity to a set of cerebral dogmas that only require unemotional compliance, not the pursuit of a loving relationship.

4. These verses teach us that Jesus’ life was defined by his relationship with his Father. At some level we choose what defines us. For example, I could choose to live as a disabled person. I could allow my disability to determine how I should view life, and control the choices I make.

Or sometimes we can be defined by the hurts of the past, and we view ourselves as victims. Or we can be defined by our success. We may look at what we have achieved and develop a heart full of pride.

But Jesus’ life teaches us, that healthy, wholesome humanity can only be defined in the context of the fatherhood of God.

What I am trying to say is, that our relationship with God the Father should be the main thing that explains who we are. As Jesus’ relationship with his father was the explanation for his life, so it should be with us.

5. The relationship Jesus had with Father gives us a practical demonstration of ‘eternal life.’

Many people have a wrong understanding of eternal life, often eternal life is viewed as a life that lasts for eternity, which is just one aspect of it, and in some ways the least important aspect. Eternal life is knowing God in an intimate way. It is a quality of life that is the result of continually growing in your knowledge and experience of God.

Over the next few days, let us look at these verses and try and understand the relationship Jesus had with his Father.

Prayer: Father give me a capacity to love you like Jesus.
Bible in a year reading plan: ; - 2 Kings 7-9
